data.title: Centerra Gold: One Of The Best Opportunities In Gold, Debt-Free And Still Undervalued
data.description:
Introduction

Despite being up ~35% since I first covered them in July 2025 and rated them a Strong Buy, Centerra Gold Inc. (NYSE:CGAU) is still miles away from my initial fair value estimation, which was based on much lower gold prices.

Now, however, with gold reaching new all-time highs over and over, the company is in an amazing financial position to reshape its future, so let’s update the valuation and take the recent developments into account.

Recent Quarter

Their Q2 was solid on the back of higher gold prices, although they still returned a negative free cash flow as a result of the high CAPEX and statutory tax plus royalty payments at Öksüt. Their Goldfield project is also advancing, and the company is still in a very good position to take advantage of the current environment.

However, they also cut their production guidance to 250,000 to 290,000 ounces and increased the AISC to $1,650 to $1,750 per ounce, mainly due to an adjustment in grades at their Mount Milligan mine. Still, this is made with an average realized gold price of $2,793 in Q2 and $2,674 per ounce in H1 as a result of the streaming agreement (e.g., the one with Royal Gold (RGLD) for Mount Milligan), as the company even cites as a reason behind the low prices.

But, as they say in the Q2 earnings conference call, they expect the second half to be the better one, with this comment about Mount Milligan:

Both gold and copper production and sales are expected to be weighted towards the second half of the year.

And this one regarding Öksüt’s second half:

We have reaffirmed our 2025 production guidance at Öksüt with production expected to be higher in the second half of the year as we access higher grade areas of the mine.

Financially, based on the latest report, we see that Centerra remains in an excellent position, with $522 million in cash and ~$922 million in total liquidity available thanks to a $400 million credit facility, while also being debt-free. Another rare thing in this market is their PB, with the $1.7 billion equity not being far from the current market cap, and if we were to account for a net asset value instead of the backward-looking balance sheet value, these assets would likely be worth significantly more.

Macro Changes

Of course, the main thing that led to gold’s jump was the Fed’s September rate cuts expected by the market, especially since Powell’s Jackson Hole remarks, and then fueled by CPI and unemployment data that came shortly after that. As I’m writing this, gold just reached a new all-time high thanks to even more rate cut expectations (2 more expected this year), which I believe are almost guaranteed in the near term, with or without Powell, whose term expires in May 2026.

This is ultimately an obvious effect of rate cuts, as they would potentially weaken the dollar, and when topped with tariffs and the risk of a hard landing leading to worsening economics, it’s no surprise to see retail investors and institutions jumping into gold, which remains the key hedge against this type of environment. Still, I don’t want to account for the current prices as a new average going forward, as you’ll see in the valuation segment, and would rather go for something like $3,300 for a margin of safety, even if I believe gold’s potential is nearly unlimited thanks to its sources of demand not being the most rational or based on actual needs (jewelry, investing, and central banks represent ~93% of gold demand).

Also, a key development to watch is Tether's expansion into gold mining and other commodities, which I believe is a move that could significantly impact interest, especially from the crypto space. This could create a new source of demand for gold, as the crypto market is generally speaking quite distinct from traditional gold buyers, with these two assets rarely being in the same portfolio due to their nature and the idea behind each of them.

As a prominent influence in the crypto space, Tether's involvement could even attract new investors to gold and gold mining stocks, which, as I mentioned before, has the potential to fuel market phenomena like fear of missing out, hype, etc., especially during periods of high inflation or recession fears, which are excellent for gold. Tether has also recently increased its stake in Adecoagro (AGRO) - my largest position - which is a sustainable producer of commodities like sugarcane, dairy, crops, and ethanol, suggesting that its interest extends beyond just gold (this move actually came earlier this year, before the expansion into gold). Although it’s still early, I believe this could shine a new light on the broader commodity market, adding the potential “Tether wild card” to virtually any commodity producer, which can lead to significant attention from the market.

And this is actually quite important in Centerra’s case. As far as I can see, this company seriously lacks the attention it deserves. Again, this is a company that’s in an exceptional financial shape and can make a ton of money for the market cap they’re traded at, benefiting from both gold and copper tailwinds and a ton of liquidity that offers optionality that can allow them to shape their future as they please. Of course, the latter is ultimately a matter of management, so execution risk remains, and just because they have the resources needed for it, this doesn’t mean they will actually deliver. They wouldn’t be the first miner to make a bad investment, and let’s not forget how their massive mine was nationalized by the Kyrgyz government in 2022 following the settlement.

Still, they are aware of their falling production at their Turkish mine, which remains a key internal risk detailed in the initial analysis, so they became quite aggressive in terms of buys, acquiring 3 stakes in smaller miners in the past year.

So, once again, the company has a ton of liquidity that can be used to expand outside of Turkey, where the mine has entered into a significant decline that is likely going to steeply fall from 2029 onwards, as they present above. They bought the Goldfield project back in 2022, which they expect to have a life of mine of about 7 years and an average annual production of 100,000 oz in peak years (2029-2032) and an AISC of $1,392/oz. This would become operational in late 2028 and fully ramp up in 2029, so enough to offset the falling Öksüt mine’s production at first.

Plus Kemess, which has the potential to be massive and is initially targeting an annual production of 250,000 gold equivalent ounces (preliminary economic assessment coming by the end of 2025). And there’s also the Thompson Creek project (this one’s molybdenum, not gold) that they say is on track for first production in the second half of 2027, which they plan to integrate with their Langeloth metallurgical facility, which processes molybdenum. To this, add the potential from whatever they will decide to do with this ~$922 million in liquidity, which is a ton of money for a company valued at nearly $2 billion even after the recent jump.

Updated Valuation

They made $298.4 million cash from operations in 2024 on ~368,000 ounces, but a realized gold price of $2,078 based on their FY24 report. If we adjust for higher realized gold prices this year and assuming 270,000 ounces of gold in 2025 based on the midpoint of the new guidance, we’re getting to roughly $330 million in OpCF. For this year’s expected CAPEX, we’re looking at about $90 million in cash flow as a result. I’ll expect the CAPEX to remain elevated for the following few years due to Thompson Creek and Goldfield and will assume a $75 million addition from 2027 onwards thanks to Thompson Creek and lower CAPEX for it (even a 10% margin on that can produce over $20 million per year, as a note), as well as another additional $50 million from lower CAPEX from 2029 onwards. I’ll assume a CAGR of 6% in the first 5 years and 4.5% in the subsequent 5 years thanks to the very high-growth potential their balance sheet and liquidity come with, plus a 3% terminal growth rate, $522 million in net cash, 204.33 million shares outstanding, and a WACC of 8.5% based on their cost of equity since there’s no debt. This returns an enterprise value of $4.3 billion and $4.82 billion in equity value after adding the net cash, meaning a price of about $23.59 per share, which is significantly below the current level even after the recent jump.

Conclusions

Overall, Centerra remains a Strong Buy for me, backed by the excellent financials and potential to turn the business around in the future, thanks to their projects, which they’re able to fund organically. It’s true that there is risk, especially due to the Turkish mine’s production falling and uncertainty about how they’ll spend the capital going forward - as they’re not known as the best in this regard - but the risk-reward remains among the best in the industry, with the company’s assets and financials alone being attractive enough for a potential buyer.

data.title: AI Infrastructure: Williams Is Beating Kinder Morgan In The Race To Power Data Centers
data.description:
Natural gas infrastructure in the United States is one of the sectors that I am most bullish on right now because it combines stagflation resistance from its long-duration, commodity-price-resistant cash flows and inflation protections with significant long-term macro growth tailwinds due to the AI boom and reindustrialization trends in the United States.

This is because natural gas will likely be the preferred energy source to power data centers, factories, and other industrial activity in the country in the coming years, as it is clean, inexpensive, and reliable.

Therefore, major natural gas infrastructure companies, especially those with existing assets strategically located near data centers and industrial centers, are positioned to benefit from significant high-returning, low-risk growth projects. This makes Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI) and Williams Companies (NYSE:WMB) particularly intriguing investment options, given that they are heavily concentrated in natural gas and are among the largest players in the United States.

In this article, I will compare them side by side and share which one I think is the better buy right now.

Williams Companies: A Natural Gas Pure Play With Growth

WMB has made strong progress in bolstering its balance sheet, as its net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio stands at a conservative 3.65x, which is a 16% improvement since 2020. As a result, today it enjoys a BBB+ credit rating from S&P and a Baa2 credit rating from Moody's.

Its debt maturities are very well laddered, with a 10.7-year weighted average term to maturity, with the vast majority of its debt maturing in 2031 and later. It also has significant liquidity thanks to a $3.75 billion credit facility that easily meets its debt maturities in any single year through the end of the decade.

WMB services 12 key supply areas and handles over one-third of U.S. natural gas through its transmission, gathering, processing, and storage assets. The company is also pursuing numerous growth projects on its Transco pipeline that should come online over the rest of the decade.

Its cash flows are very stable, and it has continued to generate adjusted EBITDA growth through various macroeconomic and commodity price cycles. Analysts project that WMB will grow its dividend per share at a 6% CAGR through the end of the decade, while its distributable cash flow per share will grow at a 9.2% CAGR over the same timeframe, powered by a 7.8% EBITDA CAGR.

Kinder Morgan: Diversified Exposure With A Big Project Backlog

KMI also has a strong balance sheet that earns it a BBB credit rating from S&P, and its 3.8x expected year-end net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio puts it in a similar leverage ballpark as WMB.

KMI is heavily focused on natural gas, though not to the same degree as WMB. About 65% of its volumes come from natural gas, with one of the largest transmission networks in the U.S. that moves 40% of U.S. natural gas production. It also generates 26% of its volumes from refined products and 9% from its CO2 business, making it one of the largest CO2 transporters and the largest independent refined products transporter and terminal operator in the U.S.

Something notable about KMI is that 95% of its cash flow is either take-or-pay, fee-based, or hedged, leaving only 5% unhedged commodity price exposure. Of that, 64% of cash flow is fee-based.

The company also has a large project backlog of $9.3 billion, with $8.6 billion tied to natural gas. These projects are being constructed at 6x EBITDA multiples or lower, which is very accretive compared to its ~11x EBITDA trading multiple.

Analysts project that KMI will grow its distributable cash flow at about a 5% CAGR through the end of the decade. That said, management has delivered relatively anemic dividend growth, with analysts expecting just a 2.9% dividend per share CAGR, despite the payout ratio being under 50%.

This indicates management prefers to reinvest in growth projects rather than accelerate dividend growth.

Side-By-Side Comparison

When comparing the two, WMB has only a slightly lower payout ratio than KMI but is expected to generate considerably more growth on a per-share basis, reflecting likely higher returns on invested capital in its growth projects.

This superior growth profile is reflected in valuations, with WMB trading at a 12.85x next-12-month EBITDA multiple versus 11.8x for KMI. Additionally, KMI offers a ~100 basis point higher yield of 4.3% compared to 3.4% for WMB.

While WMB does trade at a premium, I believe it deserves that premium, given its pure-play natural gas exposure, premier assets, higher-quality growth, and stronger dividend growth outlook.

The main risk facing both of them is execution and regulatory risk, as they are both investing aggressively in growth projects that require successful construction on time and within budget while also leaning on cooperative approvals from regulators. Both have pretty good track records in this regard, but there are no guarantees, especially when investing in so many projects at a time. Moreover, there is some interest rate risk given that they both have a considerable amount of debt on their balance sheets. However, they have brought down their leverage ratios considerably in recent years, and their stable cash flows and substantial retained distributable cash flow, along with their well-laddered debt maturities, limit their interest rate risk quite a bit.

Investor Takeaway

Overall, neither KMI nor WMB is particularly cheap at the moment, and I view both as holds. However, if I had to pick one today, I would choose WMB over KMI due to its significantly higher growth potential and better returns on invested capital. That said, I am currently allocating capital to other energy and AI infrastructure plays that offer higher yields alongside similarly compelling growth prospects, though both KMI and WMB remain on my watch list at High Yield Investor.

data.title: SLVO: A Bad Choice As Silver Could Fly Through The Double Top Resistance
data.description:
It's an exciting time for silver (SLV). It has reached 14-year highs and is approaching the "double top" resistance at the all-time high just below $50/oz. If it breaks, silver will be, quite literally, in uncharted territory. This article looks at what could happen if and when that area is reached and why the UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN (NASDAQ:SLVO) is a bad choice for this strongly trending market.

Driving Silver

Silver is an industrial metal, and the supply and demand dynamics have been in its favour for many years. Production is generally low, and demand has grown, mostly because of its use in photovoltaics and electronics.

While this is a positive, I don't think it is the primary driver of the current rally. Silver has gained +51% YTD, while silver demand has grown 33% in around 4 years.

The main driver behind this rally is gold (GLD), and specifically, central bank buying of gold.

The huge rally in gold left silver behind, resulting in a gold/silver ratio way above average.

When gold gets too expensive relative to silver, silver catches up. We don't always know exactly when this will be, but the ratio has never settled above 100, and it was above 100 in April and May this year.

Another way of looking at the gold/silver relationship was explored in one of my earlier articles from 2024. Odd as it may seem (it's explained more in the article), silver trades very much like a 2x leveraged ETF called the ProShares Ultra Gold ETF (UGL). The correlation has been high over the last 16 years, and any time gold has pulled ahead, silver has caught up eventually.

Whatever is good for gold is good for silver. The recent lag could be narrowed by silver rallying strongly or by gold pulling back. Quite likely, it will be a bit of both.

The Double Top

You have to go a long way back to find the double top with the previous peaks of $49.8 in 2011 and $48 in 1980, when the Hunt brothers cornered the silver market.

Double tops are quite common chart patterns. It's about as simple as it gets - the first peak marks resistance, and the second fails to break the resistance and sells off again.

But what happens when the double top area is reached again? A triple top? We may be about to find out, as silver has reached $44.18 and is approaching the area.

Triple tops are rare. For a triple top to form, the double top has to hold and lead to a large decline. Normally, the reactions at resistance get smaller until finally the area is broken.

What Could Happen Next

The pattern now looks more likely to develop into another common pattern, the cup and handle. You can probably see the similarities between the chart above and the chart below. The only difference is that silver has not broken out yet.

You can probably also spot the similarity between the silver chart and the chart below of gold from 2011 onwards. This again formed a cup and handle pattern and then broke out in 2024.

It's a common pattern that has good odds of success. Here's another similar chart from my article in August 2024, which called a breakout in Bitcoin (IBIT) based on the success of the gold pattern.

As many will know, Bitcoin went on to make a strong breakout in Q4 and more than doubled in price.

Of course, nothing is guaranteed, but given gold's strong lead, the strong momentum of the silver rally, and the chart pattern, I think there are good odds it breaks higher.

Silver Exposure

There are various ways to position for a rally, some better than others. SLV has outperformed the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV), and SLVO lags way behind on a total return basis. This divergence could widen if silver continues its strong rally.

SLVO is passively managed and tracks the Credit Suisse NASDAQ Silver FLOWSTM 106 Index. Its main selling point is a high yield, currently at 20.92%, but given the large rally this year, the yield is higher for those who bought in earlier.

SLVO generates its yield through selling covered calls, which provide premium but also cap the underlying asset's (SLV) participation in rallies. The covered call strategy has become very popular in recent years in the income ETFs launched by YieldMax, NEOS, Amplify, and others. Income investors are willing to overlook the lost profits in rallies and focus more on the steady income. I see the appeal, but this strategy is best suited to slow-trending or flat markets where the capped upside (lost profits) is not as painful.

In strong uptrends, the relative performance gets worse. SLVO has lagged SLV by 10% since April. Forfeiting 10% profits every 6 months just to get a 10% yield every 6 months is not a sensible choice.

If you think silver will continue its strong rally and eventually break the double top resistance, SLVO is not the best option.

Conclusions

Silver is catching up to gold and has the potential to rally to the double top resistance just under $50/oz. While this may provoke a minor reaction, triple tops are rare, and there are good odds that silver can form a cup and handle chart formation and break to new highs, just like gold and Bitcoin did in 2024.

Given the potential for a strong rally, SLV seems the best option for exposure as it has outperformed. SLVO provides a steady income but has already underperformed by 10% since April, and the conditions don't support taking a position in this fund. I am bullish on silver but rate SLVO a "sell."

data.title: Ralph Lauren's Strategy Shift - A Growth Catalyst
data.description:
Ralph Lauren (NYSE:RL) has recently hosted an Investor Day, and the company's performance and strategic initiatives have reinforced my bullish outlook on the stock. I see the company having huge potential driven by its shift towards the DTC strategy, ongoing geographic diversification, and strong margin performance supported by operational efficiency.

Strategic Shift to DTC Channels

Ralph Lauren's revenue has increased steadily by an annual average of 6.7% in the past 5 years. The company generates its revenue through three main sales channels: retail, wholesale, and licensing. In recent years, retail revenue has become increasingly significant to the company's overall revenue growth. It has become the revenue channel that has experienced the highest growth, with an annual average growth of 9% being seen. Meanwhile, the revenue growth from wholesale and licensing has been relatively volatile and modest, with negative figures being seen in some years.

Based on the graph, revenue from retail business has increased from representing 63% of Ralph Lauren's revenue 5 years ago to almost 70% now, indicating that the company is increasingly shifting toward focusing on its retail revenue.

With the strategic shift toward retail sales, I believe Ralph Lauren is better positioned to engage more directly with customers and expand its popularity across key cities globally. For instance, the company has been actively expanding its retail store count globally, with expansion plans targeting major cities such as Shanghai, Tokyo, and Paris, and having "opened up over 20 new stores in the first quarter focused on key cities." By growing its retail presence, I am seeing the company be able to sustain and continue growing its premium pricing power. This is because wholesale channels often rely on discounting, which could dilute Ralph Lauren's brand value and equity. In the latest Q1 fiscal 2026 earnings call, management reported that AUR has increased by 14%, which I believe further evidences the company's ability to generate stronger full-price sales driven by minimizing intermediary markups and discounting pressure in wholesale channels.

Besides, I believe Ralph Lauren's innovation in digital is also supporting its DTC strategy growth. The company's launch of its AI-powered virtual shopping assistant, Ask Ralph, recently in September will allow users to get personalized recommendations according to their style preferences from Ralph Lauren's inventory using prompts. Moving forward, I think this will allow the company to accelerate its DTC growth by gaining deeper insights into customers' style preferences, thereby driving higher conversion rates and online orders through more relevant and engaging shopping experiences.

Global Market Diversification

I also noted that Ralph Lauren has been diversifying its geographic revenue streams. 5 years ago, around 47% of the revenue was derived in North America. However, in 2026 Q1, this figure decreased to 38%. This shift is accompanied by an increase in revenue contributions from both Europe and Asia. Particularly, Asia has been the region experiencing the highest average annual growth of around 13% during this period. In contrast, North America has only grown modestly by 3%. I believe this trend suggests that the North American market is approaching saturation, while growth opportunities are expanding in the international market.

Therefore, I believe Ralph Lauren's efforts in investing in the international market position the company to capitalize on the growth opportunities in the global market. For instance, in Europe, Ralph Lauren has renovated its flagship stores in key cities such as Paris and London to enhance its premium consumer experience. Meanwhile, in Asia, the company has expanded its geographic footprint by opening more than 275 new stores in this region since 2022.

Among all international markets, China has been the region that has achieved the most success. In Q1 2026, management stated that China grew more than 30%, which is significantly higher than the company's overall revenue growth of around 13%. I believe this is attributable to the company's heavy investment in the brand-building marketing initiatives in China. This April, the company hosted its first-ever fashion show in Shanghai, which was a re-creation of its Spring 2025 runway show originally held in the Hamptons. Additionally, the company has also launched a documentary, "Very Ralph," in Shanghai to reinforce its connection with Chinese consumers.

The popularity of Ralph Lauren gained in China is evidenced by the impressive engagement data on Chinese social media platforms. For example, the hashtag "Ralph Lauren Shanghai Show" has gained over 795,000 views on Chinese leading social media, Xiaohongshu, while the Very Ralph documentary drew more than 30 million livestream views and 67 billion impressions across popular Chinese social media platforms. I believe these tailored engagements have allowed Ralph Lauren to showcase its brand culture and resonate with Chinese consumers, thereby allowing the brand to penetrate successfully into this relatively new market.

Another driver I'm seeing in Ralph Lauren's expansion into the Chinese market is its use of Douyin as one of the sales channels. The platform allows consumers to purchase items displayed directly during livestreams, which I believe could drive sales and engagement of Chinese consumers, as this platform has become deeply integrated into their purchasing habits. Besides, I believe using this platform also aligns with Ralph Lauren's DTC strategy, allowing the company to capture full-price sales by reaching consumers directly without relying on wholesale intermediaries.

With the success of the company's penetration into the Chinese market, I think Ralph Lauren can replicate this strategy of tailored marketing and digital innovation across other international markets. This will help the company to diversify its geographic revenue sources and achieve higher overall revenue growth despite the apparent saturation in the North American market.

Strong Margin Performance

Alongside the steady revenue expansion, the company's margin performance has been even more impressive. In 2026 Q1, Ralph Lauren's operating margin increased by 2.1% YoY, while its net margin rose by 1.67%.

Notably, the COGS margin has experienced the most significant improvement during this period, decreasing from accounting for 33% of the revenue in 2022 to 27.74% in the latest quarter. This improvement has more than offset the relatively slight increase in SG&A margin as the company increased investments in marketing and store openings in newer markets.

I believe this improvement in COGS margin can be attributed to the company's diversification into geographic regions outside of the North America. For instance, Ralph Lauren sources most of its mass-market production in Asian countries such as China, Sri Lanka, and India. Producing in these countries offers lower labour and material costs and also the proximity to high-growth key cities in Asia that the company is targeting, such as Shanghai, Tokyo, and Seoul. I believe this geographic proximity allows the company to achieve operational efficiency through more prompt inventory adjustments as well as reduced logistics cost.

As can be seen in the table, the operating margin in Asia has shown the most improvement, growing from 17.8% in 2022 to 30.7% in 2026 Q1. Meanwhile, North America's operating margin has declined from 22.8% to 20.6%. With the company's strategic shift to focus on more high-growth key cities outside of the North America, I believe Ralph Lauren is well-positioned to achieve sustainable growth in its profitability margins by leveraging the cost advantages and more efficient sales channels.

Risk: Tariff Uncertainty

Even though the company has been diversifying its revenue sources outside the North America, it still represents the company's largest market. Therefore, as an international company sourcing production from other countries, Ralph Lauren faces tariff risks that might increase its costs and further reduce its margins in the North America. I noted that the company has made an effort to mitigate this risk by shifting production to countries with lower tariffs and ensuring that no single country accounts for more than 20% of its production. Nonetheless, any unforeseen tariff increases or trade restrictions will negatively impact the company's margins, especially in North America.

Valuation

I projected the revenue by forecasting revenue growth in each region. For the financial year 2026, I used the average growth from 2022 to 2025 in each region to project their respective revenue growth. I tapered down the growth rate for the years moving forward to reflect a moderate slowdown in the growth of key markets.

Based on a DCF valuation with a WACC of 11.3% and an industry average 5-year EV/EBITDA of 16.04x of 9 companies—RL, LULU, GOOS, VFC, LEVI, CPRI, PVH, TPR, and OTCPK:BURBY—I derived a price target of $349.49, which represents an upside of 13.06%. Thus, I rate the company as a Buy.

Conclusion

Overall, I believe that Ralph Lauren is well-positioned to capitalize on its shift towards a DTC strategy, as it will allow the company to capture more full-price sales rather than having to rely on the discounts given to wholesale channels. Besides, Ralph Lauren's expansion into high-growth markets, especially China, offers faster sales growth compared to the relatively saturated market in North America, thereby driving overall revenue growth of the company. Lastly, I expect the margin improvements from higher operating margins outside North America due to lower production costs to enhance the company's margin performance moving forward as it continues to expand into international markets.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Alibaba To Boost AI Investment
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Alibaba (BABA) shares surge in Hong Kong as CEO announces AI spending boost beyond $50B plan. (00:26) Ford (F) courts riskier borrowers with lower rates for F-150 pickups: report. (01:20) Lithium Americas (LAC) skyrockets as Trump administration reportedly seeks equity stake. (03:06)

This is an abridged transcript.

Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) shares rose to their highest in nearly four years on Wednesday in Hong Kong after revealing plans to ramp up AI spending.

The company’s CEO said Alibaba now plans to boost its capital spending on artificial intelligence infrastructure beyond the originally planned 380 billion yuan ($53 billion) over the next three years.

Bloomberg reported that Eddie Wu anticipates overall investment in artificial intelligence accelerating to some $4 trillion worldwide over the next five years—and Alibaba needs to keep up.

However, he did not disclose the additional budget for AI infrastructure spending.

Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) on Tuesday unveiled its open source large language model called Qwen3-Omni, which can process text, images, audio, and video.

On Wall Street BABA is up 9% at $178.00.

Ford (NYSE:F) is trying to get more buyers for its F-150.

The Wall Street Journal reported on Tuesday, citing a spokesperson from the company, that Ford (NYSE:F) is offering lower interest rates to buyers with the weakest acceptable credit history.

This is an attempt by Ford to court these low-credit customers as it looks for a strong close to the quarter for its F-150 pickup.

The automaker highlighted its struggle with tariffs a few months ago. Ford has been largely absorbing the cost of tariffs on imported steel, aluminum, and auto parts to keep sales volume high, despite it cutting into the company's profit.

A Ford spokesman said the program isn’t expected to continue beyond the end of the month.

Lithium Americas (NYSE:LAC) +78% in pre-market action.

The surge follows a report that the Trump administration is seeking an equity stake of as much as 10% in the company as it renegotiates terms of the $2.3 billion Department of Energy loan for its Thacker Pass lithium project.

The ~$3 billion project was approved by Trump at the end of his first term, and the loan from the DoE's Loan Programs Office was closed last year by the Biden administration.

According to the report, Lithium Americas (NYSE:LAC) was scheduled to make its first draw on the loan earlier this month but Trump officials sought to renegotiate terms following concerns about the company's ability to repay given the low price of lithium.

General Motors (NYSE:GM), which invested $625 million in the mine last year for a 38% stake, has the right to buy all the lithium from the Thacker Pass project's phase 1 and part from phase 2 for 20 years, although Trump officials are now seeking a guarantee that GM will buy the metal.

data.title: Alphabet: Investing In This Longevity Leader; Could AI Double The Human Lifespan?
data.description:
I believe there is so much to like about Alphabet, as this company is involved in many very important businesses that are a daily part of life for billions of people around the planet, from our search needs to email, and increasingly more of us are finding that Gemini is one of our first real experiences with AI. In this article, I want to review parts of Alphabet's businesses that are not as well covered and known by investors because they are small now in terms of revenues, but I see these businesses as potentially being very significant growth drivers in the future. I think it is important for investors to invest in promising businesses early, and while some parts of Alphabet's businesses are looking more mature, such as traditional search, there are some relatively early-stage businesses at Alphabet that, in the future, might have the potential to profoundly impact human lives around the world. I also want to update some of the positive ongoing developments at Alphabet. With this in mind, let's take a closer look at Alphabet:

My main interest in AI is focused on two areas: one is humanoid robotics, and the other is AI-driven medical breakthroughs that can lead to longevity and cures for cancer and other diseases. I've been doing a lot of research on longevity over the past few years, and I am increasingly convinced that human beings will be able to live much longer because of the massive progress that could occur in just the next 3 to 5 years and beyond. There are credible predictions that humans will be able to essentially double their life span and live to be 150 years old in some cases. This might seem implausible based on what we have been conditioned to believe and from the obvious historical data on life expectancy. However, we know that giant tortoises often live to be 150 years old, so maybe it is time we start to think big.

Here are some even more intriguing examples: The bowhead whale can live for 200 years or more. Ocean quahog clams and Greenland sharks (the longest-living vertebrates) are said to live for 500 years or more. Antarctic glass sponges can live for between 10,000 and 15,000 years. The "immortal jellyfish" is said to be immortal as it can revert to its polyp stage to live endlessly. I believe this is proof that life is sustainable for much longer periods, and I see no reason why human life can't be greatly extended, even to 150 years old or more.

I also want to point out that some types of animals have incredible regenerative capabilities. For example, axolotls are able to regenerate limbs and even parts of their brain and other organs such as their heart, lungs, jaws, and spinal cord. Axolotls can completely regrow lost limbs within a few weeks and do so without scarring. They can also repair injured areas of their brain by creating new neurons. Researchers are studying these incredible capabilities in order to understand how to potentially achieve similar tissue repair in humans. Human beings form fibrotic scars after an injury, which prevents the regeneration that axolotls experience. If cells could be trained to regenerate instead of scarring, this could be the key to transformative regeneration for humans. I believe this is an example of the type of research that AI and quantum computing could help researchers solve. A Press Herald article discusses why scientists are so intrigued about the regenerative capabilities that axolotls have and how they could be harnessed for human beings, who also appear to have this potential; it states:

If regeneration in humans sounds like science fiction, it shouldn't. Humans have a limited ability to regenerate - for instance, newborns can regenerate heart tissue, and children can regenerate fingertips. It's likely that the circuitry is already there, but has been turned off to protect us against the threat of uncontrolled cell growth - or cancer. But what if we could temporarily reactivate this dormant circuity in adult humans long enough to repair an injury?"

These are just some examples of how much longer some animals are capable of living and that the "technology" to regenerate everything from limbs to hearts and brains is completely realistic for axolotls now and possibly for humans in the future. We just need to understand it, which is something AI could likely help us with. It seems clear that AI is going to bring massive and rapid progress to our lives in many ways, and I believe quantum computing will enhance and greatly speed up these developments. The use of AI and quantum computing could allow for research to happen at incredible speeds that are unfathomable for us to comprehend, and that is another reason why I believe we are underestimating the progress that we will see when it comes to our health and longevity even just 3 to 5 years from now.

Here is an example: In 2024, Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) (GOOGL) announced that its latest quantum computing chip, "Willow," was able to perform a computation in less than 5 minutes, which was estimated to take a traditional supercomputer about 10 septillion years to complete. This type of computational power could allow researchers to sort through massive amounts of data that could lead to gene-editing breakthroughs or blockbuster drug treatments. I wrote a bullish article on Alphabet that detailed this and other impressive milestones this company has reached, which is why it is considered by many to have achieved "quantum computing supremacy."

Alphabet is clearly making incredible progress when it comes to quantum computing, and in the not too distant future this could be used by researchers at biotech pharmaceutical companies and labs in order to develop new breakthroughs and treatments. It could also be used by Alphabet, especially since it has subsidiaries that are working on "healthspan," which is meant to improve the quality of life during our lifespan as well as longevity.

Here Are the Ways Alphabet Could Play a Role in Healthspan and Longevity

Alphabet has a full stack of incredible resources, which includes a huge hoard of cash on the balance sheet. It also has AI resources, which include DeepMind and Gemini. In addition to this, it has developed amazing quantum computing achievements such as the Willow chip. It also has some subsidiaries that are using Alphabet's DeepMind, Gemini, and Willow chip/quantum computing capabilities to accelerate healthspan and longevity, and this includes Verily Life Sciences, Calico Life Sciences, and Isomorphic Labs. Let's take a closer look at what these various endeavors are focused on and how they create a health-focused ecosystem within Alphabet:

a.) DeepMind: This is a subsidiary of Alphabet that is using AI for many purposes, and one of them is to further advance its "AI for Health" initiatives. DeepMind is focused on medical discoveries and drug development. DeepMind is known for having created AlphaFold, which is a breakthrough AI system that predicts a protein's 3D shape from its amino acid sequence. This resolves the long-standing protein folding problem, because a protein's function is based on its shape. AlphaFold has significantly expanded biological research as it is being shared freely, with the latest database release containing over 200 million entries. A recent article published by MDPI (which is an open-access publisher of scientific journals) has stated, "AlphaFold's success in accurately predicting protein structures from amino acid sequences has revolutionized various fields, from drug discovery to disease understanding." Before AlphaFold, the process of trying to predict the number of possible configurations for a single protein was considered to be a monumental task due to the astronomical level of possibilities, but AlphaFold has made it all possible, and this data is now being used to accelerate research for many diseases, including cancer.

b.) Isomorphic Labs: This subsidiary was created because of the success of DeepMind's AlphaFold, and it is focused on applying AI to the drug discovery process. Isomorphic Labs is using AlphaFold and other AI models to predict how molecules will interact and also to design new and more effective drugs and other treatments. If Isomorphic Labs has the same success in drug discovery that AlphaFold has had in predicting protein structures, this could lead to very rapid development of new treatments. Many leading pharmaceutical companies are partnering with Isomorphic Labs, and this could be a sign of the potential it holds. In 2024, Novartis (NVS) announced a strategic research collaboration with Isomorphic Labs, and based on the progress it has seen in just about one year, Novartis decided to expand this partnership in February 2025. Eli Lilly (LLY) also has a partnership deal that is "focused on discovering small molecule therapeutics against a number of undisclosed targets".

c.) Calico Life Sciences: This subsidiary of Alphabet is focused on increasing life spans and finding ways for people to live healthier lives. Calico is collaborating with institutions and pharmaceutical companies to advance this research. It has partnered with AbbVie (ABBV) to further understand age-related diseases such as cancer and neurodegeneration. Calico is focused on longevity and how it can delay the start of aging and reduce the speed of aging. Cynthia Kenyon is Calico's Vice President of Aging, and in one of her studies, she showed how a single gene could make a big difference in lifespan. Calico's website shows the potential we have to control and delay aging, and it details some of Cynthia Kenyon's work by stating:

In what has now become one of the defining studies in the field, Cynthia and her collaborators demonstrated that the rate of aging is something that can be manipulated biologically. Their study demonstrated that a single base-pair alteration or mutation in the gene daf-2 in C. elegans worms could double their lifespan. Most importantly, the worms not only lived longer, but appeared younger and healthier.

Cynthia's work, and the subsequent research by many scientists around the world, have continued to add to this knowledge base and advance the field. The genes Cynthia identified control aging in many species, including mammals. Now, no one doubts that aging science is, in fact, a field of study."

This focus on longevity at Calico Life Sciences, in collaboration with a number of major pharmaceutical companies, could become a far more significant source of revenue for Alphabet in the future, especially if there are any blockbuster treatments that come from the collaborations.

d.) Verily Life Sciences: This is a subsidiary of Alphabet that is focused on making health data more useful. This company is developing platforms to harmonize and analyze a range of datasets that come from electronic health records, genomics, and physicians' notes. Verily is working to provide AI-driven care solutions for people with chronic conditions. It is also working to develop new medical devices and software. Verily is also studying wastewater epidemiology to track health in various communities and regions, and it is developing solutions to monitor and control widespread diseases among large populations. Verily states, "We believe in the power of data and AI to profoundly improve human health."

Other Recent Developments With Alphabet

There are many recent developments with Alphabet: Meta Platforms (META) signed a 6-year, $10 billion cloud deal with Alphabet. Even bigger news is the possibility of Apple (AAPL) using Alphabet's Gemini to power Siri in the future. I see these as big votes of confidence in Alphabet. Here are four of the most significant recent developments that I see for the company:

1) On September 2, 2025, a federal judge issued remedies in the antitrust case that the Department of Justice has brought against Alphabet. The ruling was viewed as being favorable to Alphabet, as it avoided a worst-case scenario, which was a breakup of the company. The judge ruled that Alphabet would not be forced to sell off its Chrome web browser or its Android mobile operating system. This ruling means that Alphabet will not be broken up, although it will have some generally minor restrictions put on it, such as being barred from entering into exclusive search contracts for its search engine.

2) Gemini is growing in popularity, and this is putting to ease the fears that Alphabet was going to lose out to some upstart competitors such as OpenAI's ChatGPT. In fact, Gemini recently topped the app charts in both the Apple App Store and the Google Play Store. Part of this comes from the ongoing innovation with Gemini. It recently released "Nano-Banana," which is an image editing feature that has gone viral.

3) Waymo is continuing to expand and grow ridership. Waymo recently received a permit to start operating at San Francisco International Airport. Uber's (UBER) CEO has made many positive comments about the safety of Waymo's system, calling it the "right solution," which seems to be preferred by many users over Tesla's (TSLA) camera-based system.

4) In addition to all of this, Alphabet is focused on controlling and reducing expenses. It recently reported that it has eliminated about 35% of managers overseeing small teams. Alphabet has stated that over 25% of coding is now being done by AI, and that figure appears to be growing rapidly. This shows that AI has the potential to significantly reduce headcount at major tech companies and boost profit margins.

The Chart

As the chart below shows, Alphabet shares have surged in recent weeks due to a rising stock market as well as positive business developments at the company. The 50-day moving average is around $208.57, and the 200-day moving average is around $182.77. Alphabet shares are now overbought, and although I believe this stock is a strong buy for the future, it might be good to wait for pullbacks.

Potential Risk Factors

OpenAI's CEO, Sam Altman, recently suggested on CNBC that AI might have created a market bubble. If this is the case and the bubble bursts, we could see widespread selling, even with reasonably priced stocks like Alphabet. The traditional search business is likely to be impacted by AI and companies like OpenAI, but Alphabet seems to be holding onto market share for now.

Summary

I believe AI and quantum computing are going to massively increase the speed of progress when it comes to curing diseases and greatly improving our health and the length of our life span. Based on some of the examples provided in this article, I think we need to think much bigger when it comes to human life span potential. It is clear that many animals have demonstrated the ability for regeneration and incredibly long life spans. I think it is also clear that AI and quantum computing could lead to exponential and formerly unthinkable levels of progress when it comes to our health and the science of aging.

I can understand why so many investors think of Alphabet as being a search company since that is where most of its revenues come from; however, there is clearly so much more to this company. Every time I do more research on Alphabet or look into the latest developments, I just want to buy more shares of this amazing company. In one of my past articles about Alphabet, I said that I consider it to be quietly becoming the Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A), (BRK.B) of tech, AI, and robotics. It is clear that Alphabet could also play a major role in improving our health and our life span. I believe Alphabet is one of the best values when it comes to mega-cap tech stocks. I also believe that it is one of the most innovative companies. In addition to all of this, Alphabet has demonstrated breakthrough success in a range of industries, which shows impressive competency. For me, Alphabet is a must-own stock and a core long-term holding. I believe any significant pullback is a buying opportunity. Please let me know what you think about Alphabet and longevity in the comments below.

data.title: Virtus Seix U.S. Government Securities Ultra-Short Bond Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Preparing last quarter’s review, after tariffs were announced, made the set-up for 2Q look exceptionally dire. Many feared a global recession not seen since the global financial crisis (GFC), when the S&P 500® Index’s fifth biggest two-day decline since WWII arrived on April 4 and 5, and it declined -10.5%.

Then, barely a week after they were issued, the “Liberation Day” tariffs were delayed—a great relief to markets and trading partners alike. The S&P 500 rebounded on April 9, returning +9.5% in a single day. This incredible volatility was rivaled only by trading days around Black Monday (1987), the height of the GFC, and the initial COVID-19 pandemic turmoil.

Volatility was hardly limited to risk assets like stocks, as the Treasury market experienced its own whipsaw. Yields initially moved lower: The 10-year Treasury bond yield declined about 25 basis points (bps) to 4% in the week surrounding “Liberation Day.” Then the 10-year yield abruptly reversed direction, rising nearly 50 bps to 4.49% by April 11.

The shift in yields over the quarter generated modest total returns for bonds. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index generated a +1.21% total return, bringing the year-to-date tally to +4.02%. Stocks offered significant outperformance that completely reversed the first quarter’s more than 7% underperformance versus bonds. The post-“Liberation Day” low for the S&P 500 was on April 8, when its year-to-date total return hit -14.99%. By the end of the quarter, the year-to-date total return was back up to +6.2%. That unbelievable pivot was made possible by a +24.9% total return from the low on April 8, resulting in a +10.9% total return for the full quarter.

The yield curve (using the two-year and 10-year reference points) steepened by nearly 20 bps, as the challenged fiscal backdrop put pressure on the longer end of the yield curve. Intra-quarter volatility saw this curve relationship trade in a +26 bps to +64 bps range before ending June at +51 bps. Given the early July passage of President Trump’s signature fiscal package, and its likely deficit impact as estimated by the Congressional Budget Office, the backdrop for the Treasury market is likely to remain challenged, and further pressure on long-term yields could persist.

Strategy

In the second charter, short-duration agency spreads were relatively unchanged, if slightly tighter, despite the volatility related to tariff policies. Short-term interest rates were stable in the quarter, with rate-cut expectations deferred until later in the year. Main allocation changes were a slight increase in hybrid adjustable-rate mortgages (up 2.1%), and a slight decrease to fixed-rate agency commercial mortgage-backed securities (down -2.3%). There were no other meaningful allocation changes. Overall, duration ticked up slightly to 0.61 years from 0.58 years at the end of the first quarter. The Virtus Seix U.S. Government Securities Ultra-Short Bond Fund I-shares’ (Fund’s) floating-rate allocation was up slightly to 74% including cash, versus 72.8% in the first quarter. The actual floating rate, minus cash, was 72.7% in the second quarter.

Performance

The Fund generated a solid return of +1.18% in the second quarter, outperforming the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Bills 3-6 Months Index (Index) return of +1.04%. Over the past 12 months, the Fund outperformed the Index by +0.83%, with an annual return of +5.63% versus the Index’s +4.80%.

data.title: Canada's Materials Sector Strikes Gold
data.description:
By Anna Mittra

Canadian equity markets have a reputation for their sector concentration and cyclical swings, often reflecting the country’s deep ties to commodities and resource-driven growth. Over the past decade, sector performance has rotated dramatically, with Energy, Materials and Information Technology each taking their turn at the top. This constant reshuffling has reinforced the importance of diversification, while also highlighting how macroeconomic forces - from global oil shocks to pandemic-driven demand for digital solutions - have left a distinct imprint on sector performance in Canada.

Exhibit 1 highlights Information Technology leading the charts for 6 out of 10 years and stands out for its growth around the 2020 global pandemic, delivering back-to-back gains of over 60% and 80% in 2019 and 2020, respectively, as Canadian tech firms thrived in a digital-first environment. Energy, on the other hand, experienced a sharp rebound in 2021 as oil prices recovered from pandemic lows. The materials sector is rising the charts in 2024 as supported by rising gold prices and increased demand for commodities. In contrast, defensive sectors such as Consumer Staples and Utilities rarely topped the chart but consistently provided stability, often helping buffer strategies during downturns. While Financials remains Canada’s largest sector, the weight of Materials ensures that commodity trends continue to play a defining role in the market’s direction.

Exhibit 2 highlights the strong outperformance of the Materials sector year-to-date, advancing over 50% - more than double the gains of the next-best performing sector within the S&P/TSX Composite Index. The rally has been driven in large part by the strength of gold, as several of Canada’s largest mining companies, including Agnico Eagle (AEM) and Barrick (B), benefited from higher bullion prices amid ongoing macro uncertainty. Gold producers dominate the sector’s composition, representing the majority of the top constituents in the S&P/TSX Capped Materials (see Exhibit 3).

The Materials sector in Canada is heavily influenced by precious metals prices. Exhibit 4 shows that the performance of the S&P/TSX Capped Materials has historically tracked closely with movements in gold prices. The index’s capped methodology supports diversification by limiting the weight of any single issuer, while still maintaining close alignment with movements in gold prices. With gold climbing to multi-year highs, Canadian mines have driven the sector’s performance ahead of all others.

The current outperformance of Materials serves as a reminder that Canada’s market structure features listed explorers and producers of commodities - which are inputs needed for the manufacturing of semiconductors, batteries, LCD screens, electric vehicles and other high-demand goods. Contributing to a more complete picture of diversification beyond sectors like Financials and Technology, the country’s deep mining base could provide insight into the forces driving global markets today.

data.title: Virtus Seix Senior Loan ETF Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

An evolving landscape: We started the second quarter with the rollout of new tariffs that were eventually reduced, largely rolled back, or postponed. First quarter earnings were solid, overall—with some exceptions, of course. Second-quarter gross domestic product (GDP) is expected to rebound meaningfully from depressed first-quarter GDP that had been influenced by a huge influx of imports.

We continue to position the portfolio conservatively, but remain constructive on the loan market, as yields remain attractive and should continue to look attractive relative to other fixed income asset classes. We continue to expect two interest rate cuts from the U.S. Federal Reserve this year, with the first most likely coming in September. Mergers and acquisitions have increased in our market. Several companies have been acquired or announced asset sales that will pay down all, or part, of their loans. The loan market, which returned 2.36% for 2Q, looks to be on track for mid- to high-single-digit returns this year, providing healthy risk-adjusted returns.

A low-quality rally in the first two months of 2Q was met with a partial reversal of fortunes, as higher quality credits outperformed in June. According to J.P. Morgan, average loan prices rose in the quarter, with BBs up $0.33, Bs up $0.36, and CCCs up $0.86.

Fund flows into loans turned negative in 2Q, according to J.P. Morgan, although they still provided for a positive first half with a $1.7 billion inflows. Flows were negative in April and reversed in May and June. New issue activity moderated a bit, with 2Q volume at $103.9 billion, roughly a third of 1Q’s robust $337 billion. June was the strongest month after a lighter April and May.

Collateralized loan obligation (CLO) activity also slowed. The second quarter saw 220 deals totaling $102.8 billion, down from 1Q’s 296 deals totaling $142.6 billion, 4Q24’s 336 deals totaling $156 billion, and 3Q24’s 291 deals totaling $129.5 billion.

Defaults continue to be rangebound, as expected. According to J.P. Morgan, the last-12-month (LTM) default rate, including distressed exchanges, ended the month at 3.79%, up 17 basis points (bps) month-over-month, down -70 bps year-to-date, and up 69 bps year-over-year. LTM recovery rates were down slightly from 57% in the first quarter to 53.8% in the second quarter. We continue to expect defaults to remain in the 3.0%-4.0% range over the next 12-18 months, as some lower-quality companies continue to have difficulty generating free cash flow and face near-term maturities.

Leveraged loans again demonstrated "through-the-cycle" appeal in 2Q25, returning 2.85% per the J.P. Morgan Leveraged Loan Index, behind the 4.78% return from the J.P. Morgan U.S. High Yield Index and the 4.22% return from the J.P. Morgan Investment Grade Index, but materially ahead of the -1.78% return from the Russell 2000® Index.

Our bottom-up credit process identifies what we believe to be attractive opportunities in all market conditions, including the current market dislocation. Our experience through periods of market dislocation gives us the confidence to invest at attractive prices when opportunities present. We remain a flexible, nimble, value-oriented high yield manager that focuses on downside protection, which you should expect from a credit manager.

Performance

In the second quarter, the Virtus Seix Senior Loan ETF (Fund) returned 1.83% at NAV—moderately below its benchmark, the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index, which returned 2.33%. We are disappointed with the Fund’s 50 bps underperformance. According to Morningstar, we finished at the 88th percentile relative to our peers for the quarter. Over the last 12 months, our Morningstar rank placed us in the 63rd percentile.

Top Contributors

Individual Issuers: The Fund’s top positive contributors by issuer were Emergent BioSolutions (EBS) (health care), Neptune BidCo (diversified media), and Inmarsat PLC (wireless telecommunications). Sectors: The Fund’s top positive contributors by sector were energy, financials, and communication services. data.title: LyondellBasell Industries: Questionable Capital Allocation Raises Red Flags About Dividend Sustainability
data.description:
Overview

LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (NYSE:LYB) is a chemical company with a global footprint. With a product lineup that spans the plastics and petrochemical value chain, it operates across six business segments: Olefins and Polyolefins (Americas is one subsegment, while Europe and Asia round out another), Intermediates and Derivatives, Advanced Polymer Solutions, Refining, and Technology. Its products include ethylene, propylene, polyethylene, polypropylene, and a wide array of derivatives used in packaging, coatings, automotive components, and industrial applications.

The company is headquartered in Houston, Texas, but incorporated in the Netherlands. Its operations are highly capital-intensive, vertically integrated, and exposed to the price of hydrocarbon feedstocks. This exposure to commodity pricing, combined with cyclical demand from industrial end markets, creates a business profile that is volatile, operationally leveraged, and difficult to forecast through economic transitions.

At current levels, LyondellBasell trades at below five-year average levels for most metrics and offers a dividend yield above 10%. That headline yield has drawn investor attention, particularly among income-focused buyers looking for opportunities outside the traditional utility or telecom sectors. But a deeper look at the financials raises questions about sustainability and long-term capital discipline.

Investment Thesis

The bull case for LyondellBasell hinges on three core ideas: first, that the current earnings decline is cyclical rather than structural; second, that the company’s global asset base and scale give it cost advantages that will persist over time; and third, that the stock is undervalued relative to normalized earnings and asset value.

Each of those ideas has merit in isolation. The company’s operating leverage can drive earnings sharply higher in an upcycle. Management has pursued modest divestitures and cost controls. And on traditional valuation metrics such as price-to-sales and price-to-book, LYB appears cheap relative to historical averages.

However, the counterargument is equally strong. Despite multiple up-and-down cycles over the last 10 years, gross margins and operating income have shown deterioration, not just short-term volatility. Capital expenditures remain high, even during downturns. Management continues to return more capital to shareholders than it generates in free cash flow, raising questions about the sustainability of the dividend. Without a clear operational moat or pricing power, the company’s ability to outperform through a full cycle is uncertain.

For investors who specialize in cyclical timing, there may be opportunity. For those seeking a consistent, long-term compounder even in just income terms, the profile is far less attractive.

Financial Performance

On a trailing twelve-month basis, revenue stands at approximately $39 billion, down from a 2022 peak of $50 billion. That decline is not unusual for a cyclical business, but the margin contraction has been far more pronounced. Gross profit has fallen from $7 billion in 2015, a year that saw $33 billion of revenue, to just over $3.4 billion today. This alarming contraction in margin is even more apparent elsewhere, as operating income has declined from over $6 billion to $1.5 billion, while net income has collapsed to $155 million, the lowest figure reported in over a decade.

Somewhat surprisingly, the company’s share count has been reduced meaningfully over this time, from 465 million shares outstanding in 2015 to approximately 324 million today. But this buyback activity has failed to offset earnings degradation. EPS has declined from nearly $10 to under $2. Free cash flow, meanwhile, remains inconsistent due to the high fixed cost base and the inelasticity of capital expenditures.

Despite such volatility in top and bottom lines, the dividend has been increased at a CAGR of 6.24% over the last ten years. While this has certainly lined the pockets of shareholders in cash flow terms, it raises some red flags about how long this can continue on. In the most recent quarter, LyondellBasell generated $351 million in cash from operations. However, it returned $536 million to shareholders through dividends and buybacks. It also spent half a billion on CapEx, covering the shortfall with a $500 million bond issuance. With management estimating $1.7B of Capex for 2025, $1.4B for 2026, and a steady state of $1.1B per year after that, will plugging the gap with debt become a quarterly affair? If you believe that we are at the cyclical lows, then perhaps that is a saving grace. I do think it is clear, however, that this structure cannot persist forever without the debt being downgraded.

Maintaining dividends through the lean times of a cycle is one thing, but to keep buying back shares while cutting costs elsewhere and raising debt to invest in CapEx seems a bit bewildering to me. This approach, borrowing to augment capital returns, has yet to trip any breakers because the company's debt to EBITDA has remained reasonable, and their overall debt load has not looked substantial. However, it is easy to see how it could balloon out of control if managed with this status quo.

Valuation

At face value, LYB appears attractively priced. It trades at approximately 0.45 times trailing revenue and just about 1.5 times book value. These levels are below the company’s five- and ten-year historical averages. On a price-to-earnings basis, the stock looks expensive due to depressed earnings, but that metric is largely unhelpful in evaluating cyclical industrials at the bottom of a downcycle.

If one assumes that revenue and margins revert to historical norms, there may be material upside embedded in the current share price. But this assumption requires confidence in the underlying structural health of the business. The last several years have not supported that view. Gross margins are down materially. Operating leverage has cut both ways. And there is no clear evidence that prior peak earnings are repeatable under current cost structures. Criticism aside, the shares are at an objectively attractive valuation, as normally they trade closer to .75 times sales.

The asset base is large and diverse, and the book value includes substantial real estate and capital equipment. But given ongoing pressures on pricing and throughput, it is unclear whether those assets are generating sufficient returns. On a cash basis, the business has been capital-destructive in recent periods. Until that trend reverses, valuation metrics will remain compromised.

Risks

The core risks facing LYB are tied to macroeconomic conditions, energy prices, and capital allocation decisions. Demand for its products is sensitive to construction, automotive production, packaging, and other industrial verticals. These sectors have all faced headwinds from rising interest rates, slowing global growth, and tariff uncertainty. It appears that the company is unfortunately in the crosshairs of many of the worst aspects of the current macro environment.

On the input side, the company’s cost base is tied closely to hydrocarbon feedstocks. Spikes in oil or natural gas prices can compress margins quickly. While management emphasizes geographic cost advantages, global commodity convergence makes those advantages temporary at best.

The most pressing risk, in my opinion, however, is internal. The company’s ongoing commitment to dividends and buybacks, even during periods of weak earnings, creates balance sheet pressure. Debt issuance may be sustainable for now, but in the event of a prolonged downcycle or a sharp demand contraction from the current tepid level, the company may be forced to cut distributions or sell more assets to maintain liquidity. Such a move would likely reset investor expectations and rerate the stock lower. So far, the management team has signaled confidence in a reversion to normal across all ups and downs the last 10 years, continuing to raise their cost of capital (via the dividend) each year.

There is also the risk of misjudging where LYB is in the cycle. If the recent earnings declines reflect structural changes in the industry rather than temporary softness, then the stock is not cheap; it’s mispriced. That possibility should not be dismissed lightly given the multi-year margin trends.

Technical Analysis

From a technical standpoint, LYB remains in a clear downtrend. The stock briefly broke out above descending resistance in mid-July but reversed sharply, coinciding with its August earnings report. Shares dropped from $65 to below $50 in a matter of days, indicating that investor confidence remains fragile.

Price action since then has been choppy. The stock is now hovering near the upper range of its descending channel but has failed to reclaim previous support zones. Unless LYB can break decisively above this line with a fundamental catalyst or a macro shift, the chart suggests continued downside risk in the near term, which could tumble it to multi-year lows. I find the current price tenuous on a technical basis, as it is close to the upper bound of the downtrend line, and there is little to suggest that a near-term catalyst will break it out anytime soon, suggesting that more downside is likely in the immediate term.

Conclusion: LYB Earns an "Avoid" Grade

LyondellBasell is a large, globally integrated player in the chemical space, but its financial performance over the past decade points to noteworthy challenges. While the industry has faced its ups and downs, the management team appears to have prioritized shareholders above most other aspects of the business, which doesn't always pan out well in the long run. Capital returns have outpaced cash generation. And the dividend, while headline-attractive, rests on a fragile foundation. This is a clear example of a value trap in my opinion, and while it may scorch back up to earth when the upcycle returns, it will only mask the underlying weakness that remains.

The valuation screens as cheap, but that conclusion depends entirely on the assumption of a cyclical rebound in both earnings and margins. If that rebound occurs, LYB may offer strong returns from current levels. If it does not, the company is left with declining profitability, rising debt, and limited flexibility.
